topics.title: Hematopoietic Stem Cells
topics.content:
Hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) are defined by two cardinal properties: self-renewal and multipotent differentiation into all mature blood cell lineages. They reside in the bone marrow microenvironment (stem cell niche), which includes osteoblasts, endothelial cells, mesenchymal stromal cells, and CXCL12-abundant reticular (CAR) cells.

Hematopoiesis Hierarchy

HSC → Common Myeloid Progenitor (CMP) → granulocytes, monocytes, erythrocytes, megakaryocytes HSC → Common Lymphoid Progenitor (CLP) → B cells, T cells, NK cells

CD34+ is the key surface marker used to identify and enumerate HSCs for clinical purposes (e.g., stem cell harvest for transplant).

Key Hematopoietic Growth Factors
Growth FactorTarget LineageClinical Use
Erythropoietin (EPO)Red blood cellsAnemia of CKD, MDS
Thrombopoietin (TPO)Megakaryocytes/PlateletsTPO-RAs for ITP, AA
G-CSFNeutrophilsFebrile neutropenia, stem cell mobilization
GM-CSFGranulocytes + MonocytesPost-chemotherapy recovery

topics.title: Iron Deficiency & Anemias of Chronic Disease
topics.content:
Body Iron Distribution (Table 97-1)
CompartmentIron (mg)
Hemoglobin2500
Myoglobin/enzymes400
Transferrin-bound3–7
Ferritin stores100–1000
Causes of Iron Deficiency (Table 97-2)
  • Chronic blood loss: GI (most common in men/postmenopausal women), menstrual (most common in premenopausal women)
  • Inadequate intake: Infants, elderly, restrictive diets
  • Malabsorption: Celiac disease, gastrectomy, H. pylori, inflammatory bowel disease
  • Increased requirements: Pregnancy, growth spurts, lactation
Iron Studies — Differential Diagnosis (Table 97-3)
TestIron DeficiencyACDBoth (IDA + ACD)
Serum iron↓↓↓
TIBC↑↓↓
Transferrin saturation↓ (<18%)↓↓
Ferritin↓ (<15 μg/L)30–200↓ to normal
sTfR↑Normal↑
Clinical Pearl: Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant — in the setting of inflammation, a ferritin <100 μg/L suggests coexistent iron deficiency. The sTfR/log ferritin index helps distinguish IDA from ACD when ferritin is equivocal.
DDx of Microcytic Anemia (Table 97-4)
CauseKey Feature
Iron deficiencyLow ferritin, high TIBC, ↑RDW
ACDElevated ferritin, low TIBC
Thalassemia traitTarget cells, normal/↑ferritin, low RDW, Mentzer index <13
Sideroblastic anemiaRing sideroblasts on BM, ↑iron stores
Lead poisoningBasophilic stippling, ↑FEP, abdominal pain
Oral Iron Preparations (Table 97-5)
PreparationTotal DoseElemental Iron
Ferrous sulfate 325 mgTID65 mg
Ferrous gluconate 325 mgTID36 mg
Ferrous fumarate 325 mgTID106 mg
  • Give on empty stomach with vitamin C to enhance absorption
  • Response: Reticulocyte rise in 1–2 weeks; Hb rises 1–2 g/dL per month
  • Continue for 3–6 months after Hb normalization to replenish stores
Parenteral Iron

Indicated when oral iron fails/not tolerated, malabsorption exists, or ongoing losses exceed oral replacement capacity. Options: IV iron sucrose, ferric carboxymaltose, ferumoxytol.

Hepcidin — Master Regulator of Iron Homeostasis

Hepcidin (produced by liver) blocks ferroportin on enterocytes and macrophages → iron sequestration. Upregulated by inflammation (IL-6), iron loading. Downregulated by iron deficiency, hypoxia, erythropoietic drive. This mechanism explains the pathophysiology of ACD.

Anemia of Chronic Kidney Disease
  • Primarily due to EPO deficiency
  • Treat with ESAs (epoetin alfa, darbepoetin alfa) — target Hb 10–11.5 g/dL (NOT >13 → ↑CV risk)
  • Functional iron deficiency common: give IV iron if ferritin <500 and TSAT <30%
Anemia of Chronic Inflammation

Four mechanisms: hepcidin-mediated iron sequestration, blunted EPO response, shortened RBC survival, impaired erythroid progenitor proliferation.

topics.title: Hemoglobin Disorders
topics.content:
Sickle Cell Disease

Pathophysiology: Deoxygenated HbS (β6 Glu→Val) polymerizes → rigid sickled cells → vaso-occlusion + chronic hemolysis.

SCD Genotypes (Table 98-2)
GenotypeSeverity
HbSSSevere
HbSCModerate
HbS/β⁰-thalSevere
HbS/β⁺-thalMild–moderate
SCD Complications (Table 98-3)
  • Vaso-occlusive crises: Bone pain (#1 reason for ED visits), triggered by dehydration, cold, infection, hypoxia
  • Acute chest syndrome (ACS): Leading cause of death — fever, new pulmonary infiltrate, respiratory symptoms. Rx: exchange transfusion + antibiotics + supplemental O₂
  • Stroke: 11% in children by age 20; transcranial Doppler screening → chronic transfusion (target HbS <30%)
  • Splenic sequestration: Acute splenomegaly + Hb drop ≥2 g/dL; emergency transfusion
  • Aplastic crisis: Parvovirus B19 → transient red cell aplasia; reticulocytes absent
  • Priapism, avascular necrosis (femoral head), pulmonary hypertension, CKD, proliferative retinopathy
SCD Treatment
AgentMechanismIndication
Hydroxyurea↑ HbF production≥3 crises/yr, history of ACS; ↓ crises by 50%
VoxelotorHbS polymerization inhibitor (↑ Hb O₂ affinity)Chronic anemia
CrizanlizumabAnti-P-selectin mAb↓ vaso-occlusive crises
L-glutamine↓ oxidative stress in sickle RBCs↓ pain crises
TransfusionDilute HbSACS, stroke prevention (target HbS <30%)
HSCTCurativeHLA-matched sibling donor
Gene therapyLentiGlobin (betibeglogene autotemcel)Curative potential
Beta-Thalassemia (Table 98-4)
TypeGenotypeHb PatternClinical
Minor/Traitβ/β⁺ or β/β⁰↑ HbA2 (>3.5%)Mild microcytic anemia
Intermediaβ⁺/β⁺ or β⁺/β⁰VariableModerate anemia, no regular transfusion
Majorβ⁰/β⁰Mostly HbFSevere, transfusion-dependent
  • Major treatment: Chronic transfusion + iron chelation (deferasirox PO daily, deferoxamine SC/IV, deferiprone PO). Luspatercept (activin receptor ligand trap) for transfusion-dependent β-thal. HSCT is curative.
Alpha-Thalassemia
Genes DeletedNameClinical
1 (-α/αα)Silent carrierNormal
2 (-α/-α or --/αα)TraitMild microcytic anemia
3 (--/-α)HbH diseaseModerate hemolytic anemia
4 (--/--)Hydrops fetalisFatal in utero (Hb Bart's = γ₄)

topics.title: Megaloblastic Anemias
topics.content:
Cobalamin (B12) Deficiency — Causes
  • Pernicious anemia (#1 cause in adults — anti-IF antibodies, anti-parietal cell antibodies)
  • Gastrectomy/bariatric surgery
  • Ileal resection/Crohn's disease
  • Tropical sprue
  • Fish tapeworm (Diphyllobothrium latum)
  • Strict vegan diet (no animal products)
  • Nitrous oxide abuse (oxidizes B12)
  • Pancreatic insufficiency
Folate Deficiency — Causes (Table 99-5)
  • Poor diet: Alcoholism, elderly, poverty
  • Increased requirements: Pregnancy, chronic hemolysis, malignancy, exfoliative dermatitis
  • Malabsorption: Celiac disease, tropical sprue
  • Drugs: Methotrexate (DHF reductase inhibitor), trimethoprim, phenytoin, sulfasalazine
Diagnostic Differentiation
TestB12 DeficiencyFolate Deficiency
Serum B12↓ (<148 pmol/L)Normal
Serum folateNormal or ↑↓
RBC folate↓↓
Methylmalonic acid↑↑Normal
Homocysteine↑↑
Key distinguishing test: Methylmalonic acid — elevated ONLY in B12 deficiency.
Clinical Features
  • Hematologic: Megaloblastic anemia (MCV >100 fL), hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes), oval macrocytes, pancytopenia possible
  • GI: Glossitis (smooth, beefy-red tongue)
  • Neurologic (B12 ONLY): Subacute combined degeneration — posterior columns (loss of vibration/proprioception) + lateral corticospinal tracts (spasticity, Babinski+). Peripheral neuropathy. Dementia. May occur WITHOUT anemia.
Treatment
  • B12: Hydroxocobalamin 1000 μg IM daily × 1 week → weekly × 4 weeks → monthly for life
  • Folate: 5 mg/day PO
  • CRITICAL: ALWAYS rule out B12 deficiency before treating with folate alone — folate corrects the anemia but masks ongoing neurological damage from B12 deficiency

topics.title: Hemolytic Anemias
topics.content:
Classification (Table 100-1)
IntracorpuscularExtracorpuscular
InheritedMembrane (HS, HE), Enzyme (G6PD, PK), Hemoglobin disorders—
AcquiredPNHAIHA, TTP/HUS, mechanical (valves), DIC, infections (malaria)
Clinical Pearl: All intracorpuscular causes are inherited EXCEPT PNH (acquired intracorpuscular).
Laboratory Evidence of Hemolysis
MarkerDirection
LDH↑
Indirect bilirubin↑
Reticulocyte count↑
Haptoglobin↓ (absent in intravascular)
Urine urobilinogen↑
Urine hemosiderin+ (intravascular)
Hereditary Spherocytosis (HS)
  • Inheritance: AD (75%), AR (25%)
  • Genes: ANK1 (ankyrin), SLC4A1 (band 3), SPTA1/SPTB (spectrins), EPB41, EPB42
  • Lab: MCHC >34 g/dL, RDW >14%, spherocytes on smear
  • Diagnosis: EMA binding test (flow cytometry) — most sensitive and specific. Osmotic fragility test (less used now).
  • Treatment: Folic acid supplementation. Splenectomy for severe disease (after age 6; vaccinate against encapsulated organisms — pneumococcus, meningococcus, H. influenzae — before surgery).
G6PD Deficiency
  • Inheritance: X-linked recessive — most common enzymopathy worldwide
  • Pathophysiology: ↓ NADPH → ↓ reduced glutathione → oxidative damage to Hb → Heinz bodies → bite cells
G6PD Triggers (Table 100-5)
  • Drugs: Primaquine, dapsone, sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin, methylene blue
  • Foods: Fava beans (favism)
  • Other: Infections (#1 trigger), DKA
G6PD Subtypes
TypeActivityHemolysisGeography
A-10–60%Self-limited (young RBCs spared)African
Mediterranean<1%Severe, not self-limitedMediterranean, Middle Eastern

Diagnostic Pearl: G6PD levels may be falsely normal during acute crisis (reticulocytes/young RBCs have higher G6PD) → retest 2–3 months after episode.

Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (AIHA)
FeatureWarm AIHACold Agglutinin Disease (CAD)
AntibodyIgGIgM
DATIgG ± C3C3 only
Temperature37°C<37°C
CausesIdiopathic, SLE, CLL, drugs (methyldopa)Mycoplasma, EBV, lymphoproliferative disorders
TreatmentPrednisone 1 mg/kg → rituximab → splenectomyAvoid cold exposure, rituximab ± bendamustine
Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH)
  • Mutation: PIGA gene (X-linked, acquired somatic mutation in HSC) → absent GPI-anchored proteins (CD55, CD59)
  • Classic triad: Intravascular hemolytic anemia + thrombosis (Budd-Chiari syndrome, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis) + cytopenias (BM failure overlap)
  • Diagnosis: Flow cytometry — FLAER assay (gold standard); absence of CD55/CD59
  • Treatment: Eculizumab or ravulizumab (anti-C5 complement mAb). Meningococcal vaccination required before starting complement inhibitors. HSCT for severe BM failure.

topics.title: Aplastic Anemia & MDS
topics.content:
Pancytopenia — Differential Diagnosis (Table 102-1)

Aplastic anemia, MDS, acute leukemia, megaloblastic anemia, hypersplenism, SLE, HIV, TB, visceral leishmaniasis, hairy cell leukemia, myelofibrosis

Aplastic Anemia — Classification
SeverityCriteria
ModerateHypocellular BM + cytopenias not meeting severe criteria
Severe (SAA)BM cellularity <25% + ≥2 of: ANC <500, platelets <20,000, reticulocytes <1% (or absolute <60,000)
Very SevereSAA criteria + ANC <200
Etiology (Table 102-3)
  • Idiopathic: >50% (presumed autoimmune T-cell mediated)
  • Drugs: Chloramphenicol, NSAIDs, gold, carbamazepine, phenytoin
  • Chemicals: Benzene, insecticides
  • Viruses: Hepatitis (seronegative), EBV, HIV, parvovirus B19
  • Radiation, pregnancy, PNH overlap
Treatment Algorithm
PatientFirst-lineResponse
Age <40 + matched sibling donorAllogeneic HSCTCure rate 80–90%
Others / no donorHorse ATG + cyclosporine + eltrombopagResponse ~85% with eltrombopag added upfront
RefractoryMatched unrelated donor HSCT or repeat ISTVariable
Myelodysplastic Syndromes (MDS)

Defining features: Ineffective hematopoiesis, dysplasia in ≥1 lineage, cytopenias, risk of AML transformation

WHO Classification (Table 102-5)
SubtypeKey Features
MDS-SLDSingle lineage dysplasia, <5% blasts
MDS-MLDMulti-lineage dysplasia, <5% blasts
MDS-RSRing sideroblasts ≥15% (or ≥5% with SF3B1 mutation)
MDS-EB15–9% BM blasts
MDS-EB210–19% BM blasts
MDS-del(5q)Isolated del(5q), <5% blasts, responds to lenalidomide
IPSS-R Risk Stratification (Table 102-6)

Variables: Cytogenetics + BM blast % + Hb + ANC + Platelets → Score → Very low / Low / Intermediate / High / Very high risk

Risk GroupMedian SurvivalAML Transformation
Very low8.8 yearsVery low
Low5.3 yearsLow
Intermediate3.0 yearsIntermediate
High1.6 yearsHigh
Very high0.8 yearsVery high
MDS Treatment
RiskTreatment
Low riskEPO ± G-CSF (if EPO <500), lenalidomide for del(5q) → 67% transfusion independence
High riskAzacitidine or decitabine (hypomethylating agents); HSCT is the only curative option

topics.title: Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
topics.content:
WHO MPN Classification (Table 103-1)

CML, Polycythemia Vera (PV), Essential Thrombocythemia (ET), Primary Myelofibrosis (PMF), Chronic Neutrophilic Leukemia (CNL), Chronic Eosinophilic Leukemia (CEL), MPN-Unclassifiable

Driver Mutations in Ph-Negative MPNs
MutationPVETPMF
JAK2 V617F>95%50%55%
JAK2 exon 12<5%——
CALR—30%36%
MPL—8%4%
Triple-negative—12%5%
Polycythemia Vera (PV)

Diagnosis (WHO): Hb >16.5 g/dL (M) / >16 g/dL (F) OR Hct >49% / >48% + JAK2 mutation + low EPO ± BM biopsy (panmyelosis)

Causes of Erythrocytosis (Table 103-2)
CategoryExamples
RelativeDehydration, stress (Gaisböck syndrome)
PrimaryPolycythemia vera
Secondary — appropriate EPOHypoxia (COPD, high altitude, sleep apnea, R→L shunt), high-affinity Hb
Secondary — inappropriate EPORenal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, cerebellar hemangioblastoma, uterine fibroids

Treatment:

  • All patients: Phlebotomy (target Hct <45%) + low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily
  • High-risk (age >60 OR prior thrombosis): Add hydroxyurea
  • Complications: Thrombosis (#1 cause of death), progression to myelofibrosis (15%), AML (5–10%), aquagenic pruritus (treat with antihistamines, ruxolitinib)
Primary Myelofibrosis (PMF)

Blood smear: Teardrop cells (dacrocytes), leukoerythroblastic picture (nucleated RBCs + immature granulocytes) BM: Dry tap on aspirate, reticulin/collagen fibrosis on biopsy Massive splenomegaly + constitutional symptoms (night sweats, weight loss, fevers)

DIPSS-Plus Scoring (Tables 103-4/5)

Risk factors: Age >65, Hb <10, WBC >25,000, circulating blasts ≥1%, constitutional symptoms, platelets <100,000, transfusion dependence, unfavorable karyotype

RiskMedian Survival
Low15.4 years
Intermediate-16.5 years
Intermediate-22.9 years
High1.3 years

Treatment:

  • Symptomatic splenomegaly/constitutional symptoms: Ruxolitinib (JAK1/2 inhibitor) — ↓ spleen size, improves symptoms and survival
  • Curative: HSCT (for young, high-risk patients)
  • Anemia: Danazol, ESAs, luspatercept, transfusions
Essential Thrombocythemia (ET)
  • Diagnosis: Sustained platelets >450,000 + driver mutation (JAK2/CALR/MPL) + BM showing megakaryocyte proliferation + exclusion of other MPNs
  • Risk: Thrombosis (arterial > venous). Acquired von Willebrand disease if platelets >1 million (↓ VWF activity → paradoxical bleeding)
  • Treatment: Low-dose aspirin for all. Add hydroxyurea or anagrelide if high-risk (age >60, prior thrombosis, platelets >1.5 million).

topics.title: Acute Myeloid Leukemia
topics.content:
AML WHO 2016 Classification (Table 104-2)
CategorySubtypes
AML with recurrent genetic abnormalitiest(8;21) RUNX1-RUNX1T1, inv(16) CBFB-MYH11, APL t(15;17) PML-RARA, t(9;11) MLL, t(6;9), inv(3), t(1;22), NPM1 mutated, biallelic CEBPA, BCR-ABL1
AML with MDS-related changesDysplasia ≥2 lineages or prior MDS
Therapy-related AMLPrior alkylating agents (5–7 yr) or topoisomerase II inhibitors (1–3 yr)
AML NOSWithout recurrent genetic abnormalities
ELN 2017 Risk Stratification (Table 104-3)
RiskGenetic Abnormality
Favorablet(8;21), inv(16), NPM1 mutated without FLT3-ITD, biallelic CEBPA
IntermediateNPM1 mut + FLT3-ITD, wild-type NPM1 + FLT3-ITD (low allelic ratio), t(9;11)
AdverseComplex karyotype (≥3 abnormalities), monosomal karyotype, -5/del(5q), -7, -17/abn(17p), t(6;9), t(v;11q23.3) non-t(9;11), TP53 mut, RUNX1 mut, ASXL1 mut
Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia — APL [t(15;17)]
  • ATRA + ATO (arsenic trioxide) for low/intermediate risk → CR near 100%
  • Differentiation syndrome: Fever, dyspnea, pulmonary infiltrates, weight gain, pleural/pericardial effusions → treat with dexamethasone 10 mg BID
  • DIC common at presentation — do NOT delay ATRA for confirmatory cytogenetics
  • Auer rods (bundles = "faggot cells") pathognomonic
Standard AML Induction: "7+3"

Cytarabine continuous infusion × 7 days + anthracycline (daunorubicin or idarubicin) × 3 days → CR 60–80%

Novel Targeted Therapies (Table 104-6)
TargetDrugSetting
FLT3 mutationMidostaurin (+ 7+3)Newly diagnosed
FLT3 mutationGilteritinibRelapsed/refractory
IDH1IvosidenibRelapsed/newly diagnosed
IDH2EnasidenibRelapsed
BCL-2Venetoclax + azacitidineOlder/unfit patients
CD33Gemtuzumab ozogamicin (ADC)Favorable-risk AML

topics.title: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia
topics.content:
Pathogenesis: Philadelphia chromosome — t(9;22)(q34;q11.2) → BCR-ABL1 fusion gene → constitutive tyrosine kinase activity → uncontrolled myeloid proliferation

Triphasic disease: Chronic phase (85% at diagnosis) → Accelerated phase (10–19% blasts, basophils ≥20%) → Blast crisis (≥20% blasts, like acute leukemia)

Lab features: WBC ↑↑↑ (often >100,000) with full spectrum of myeloid maturation (left shift), basophilia, eosinophilia, low LAP score (vs leukemoid reaction which has high LAP)

TKI Therapy (Table 105-2)
TKIDoseGenerationNotes
Imatinib400 mg QD1stStandard first-line
Dasatinib100 mg QD2ndPleural effusions
Nilotinib300 mg BID2ndCV risk, pancreatitis
Bosutinib400 mg QD2ndDiarrhea, hepatotoxicity
Ponatinib45 mg QD3rdCovers T315I, vascular occlusion risk
Asciminib40 mg BIDSTAMPCovers T315I, myristoyl pocket binder
Response Milestones
TimeTarget
3 monthsBCR-ABL1 ≤10% (IS)
6 monthsBCR-ABL1 ≤1%
12 monthsMMR: BCR-ABL1 ≤0.1%; CCyR: 0% Ph+ metaphases

T315I "gatekeeper" mutation: Resistant to all 1st/2nd-gen TKIs → only responds to ponatinib or asciminib

Treatment-free remission (TFR): Can attempt TKI discontinuation if sustained DMR (MR4.5, BCR-ABL1 ≤0.0032%) for ≥2 years. ~50% maintain remission off therapy.

topics.title: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
topics.content:
Immunologic Subtypes (Table 106-2)
SubtypeFrequencyMarkers
B-ALL76%CD19, CD22, CD10 (CALLA), CD79a
T-ALL24%CD3, CD7, CD5, CD2
Key Cytogenetics
TranslocationGenePrognosis
t(9;22) BCR-ABL (Ph+)BCR-ABL1Poor (25% of adult B-ALL) → add TKI
t(12;21)ETV6-RUNX1Good (children)
t(4;11)MLLPoor (infants)
Hyperdiploidy >50—Good (children)
Hypodiploidy <44—Very poor
Treatment Phases
PhaseKey Drugs
Pre-phaseDexamethasone + cyclophosphamide (tumor lysis prevention)
InductionVincristine + prednisone/dexamethasone + anthracycline + asparaginase ± TKI if Ph+
ConsolidationHigh-dose methotrexate, cytarabine
Maintenance (2–2.5 years)Daily 6-mercaptopurine + weekly methotrexate
  • CNS prophylaxis: Intrathecal methotrexate ± cytarabine ± radiation — mandatory in ALL
  • MRD: Minimal residual disease assessment is critical. MRD-positive after induction → high risk → consider HSCT
Novel Immunotherapies
AgentMechanismIndication
BlinatumomabBispecific anti-CD3/CD19 (BiTE)Relapsed/refractory B-ALL, MRD+
Inotuzumab ozogamicinAnti-CD22 ADCRelapsed/refractory B-ALL
TisagenlecleucelCAR-T cells (anti-CD19)Relapsed B-ALL (age ≤25)

topics.title: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
topics.content:
Diagnostic Criteria (Table 111-1)
EntityCriteria
MGUSM-protein <3 g/dL, BM plasma cells <10%, no CRAB
SMMM-protein ≥3 g/dL OR BM plasma cells 10–60%, no CRAB or SLiM
Multiple MyelomaBM plasma cells ≥10% + CRAB or SLiM-CRAB
CRAB Criteria
  • Calcium >11 mg/dL
  • Renal insufficiency (Cr >2 mg/dL or CrCl <40)
  • Anemia (Hb <10 g/dL or ↓2 g/dL from baseline)
  • Bone lesions (lytic lesions on skeletal survey, CT, or PET-CT)
SLiM Biomarkers (myeloma-defining events without CRAB)
  • Sixty: BM plasma cells ≥60%
  • Li: Involved/uninvolved serum free light chain ratio ≥100
  • M: >1 focal lesion on MRI (≥5 mm each)
ISS Staging (Table 111-3)
Stageβ2-MicroglobulinAlbuminMedian Survival
I<3.5 mg/L≥3.5 g/dL62 months
IINeither I nor III—44 months
III≥5.5 mg/L—29 months

R-ISS adds: LDH (normal vs elevated) + high-risk cytogenetics: del(17p), t(4;14), t(14;16)

Treatment (Table 111-4)
SettingRegimen
Transplant-eligible (<70, fit)Induction: RVd (lenalidomide + bortezomib + dexamethasone) × 4 cycles → ASCT → lenalidomide maintenance
Transplant-ineligibleVRd or DRd (daratumumab + lenalidomide + dexamethasone) until progression
Drug Classes in Myeloma
ClassAgents
IMiDsThalidomide, lenalidomide, pomalidomide
Proteasome inhibitors (PIs)Bortezomib (peripheral neuropathy), carfilzomib (cardiac), ixazomib (oral)
Monoclonal antibodiesDaratumumab (anti-CD38), elotuzumab (anti-SLAMF7)
BCMA-targetedBelantamab mafodotin (ADC), CAR-T: idecabtagene vicleucel (ide-cel), ciltacabtagene autoleucel (cilta-cel)
Waldenström's Macroglobulinemia (Lymphoplasmacytic Lymphoma)
  • IgM monoclonal gammopathy + MYD88 L265P mutation (>90%)
  • Hyperviscosity syndrome: Blurred vision, headache, mucosal bleeding → plasmapheresis (emergent)
  • Treatment: Ibrutinib (BTK inhibitor), rituximab-based regimens (avoid rituximab monotherapy initially — IgM flare)

topics.title: Amyloidosis
topics.content:
Classification (Table 112-1)
TypePrecursor ProteinAssociated Disease
ALImmunoglobulin light chainPlasma cell dyscrasia
AASerum amyloid AChronic inflammation (RA, FMF, TB, bronchiectasis)
ATTR wild-typeTransthyretin (normal)Senile cardiac amyloidosis (age >70)
ATTR hereditaryMutant transthyretinFamilial (Val30Met most common)
Aβ2Mβ2-microglobulinDialysis-related (carpal tunnel, arthropathy)
Diagnosis
  • Congo red staining → apple-green birefringence under polarized light
  • Biopsy sites: Abdominal fat pad aspirate (sensitivity ~80%), rectal biopsy, or affected organ biopsy
  • Typing: Mass spectrometry (gold standard) or immunohistochemistry — CRITICAL to determine type before treatment
AL Amyloidosis
  • Cardiac involvement = #1 cause of death (restrictive cardiomyopathy, low-voltage ECG, thickened walls on echo)
  • Staging: BNP/NT-proBNP and troponin (Mayo staging)
  • Treatment: High-dose melphalan/ASCT if fit; otherwise CyBorD (cyclophosphamide + bortezomib + dexamethasone) or Dara-VCd (daratumumab + VCd)
AA Amyloidosis
  • Treat the underlying inflammatory condition
  • Colchicine for FMF (prevents AA amyloidosis)
ATTR Amyloidosis
AgentMechanismIndication
TafamidisTTR stabilizerATTR-cardiomyopathy (approved, ↓ mortality)
PatisiransiRNA (TTR gene silencer)ATTR-polyneuropathy
InotersenAntisense oligonucleotide (TTR)ATTR-polyneuropathy

topics.title: Disorders of Platelets & Vessel Wall
topics.content:
Thrombocytopenia — Evaluation Framework
MechanismExamples
↓ ProductionBM failure (AA, MDS), infiltration (leukemia, mets), infection (HIV, HCV), drugs, B12/folate deficiency
↑ DestructionITP, TTP, HUS, HIT, DIC, HELLP
SequestrationSplenomegaly (portal HTN, Gaucher's)
DilutionalMassive transfusion
Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT)
FeatureType IType II
MechanismNon-immune, direct heparin effectImmune: IgG anti-PF4/heparin complex
OnsetDays 1–4Days 5–14 (or <1 day if prior heparin exposure)
Platelet countRarely <100K, mild drop50% drop from baseline
ThrombosisNoYES — arterial and venous (20–50%)
ActionBenign, can continue heparinSTOP all heparin immediately

4T Score: Thrombocytopenia + Timing + Thrombosis + oTher causes excluded

HIT Type II Treatment:

  • STOP all heparin (including flushes, heparin-coated catheters)
  • Start alternative anticoagulant: argatroban (hepatic metabolism — use in renal failure), bivalirudin, fondaparinux
  • Do NOT give warfarin until platelets recover (>150K) — risk of warfarin-induced venous limb gangrene
  • Do NOT give platelet transfusions — fuels thrombosis
Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP)
  • Diagnosis of exclusion — no single confirmatory test
  • Clinical: Mucocutaneous bleeding (petechiae, purpura, epistaxis, menorrhagia). Symptoms usually when platelets <30,000
  • Smear: Large platelets (young), otherwise normal

Treatment Ladder:

LineTreatmentNotes
ObservationIf platelets >30K without bleedingWatch and wait
1st linePrednisone 1 mg/kg × 2–4 weeks + taper60–80% initial response
UrgentIVIg 1–2 g/kg over 1–5 daysRapid ↑ platelets (24–48h)
Urgent (Rh+)Anti-Rh(D) immunoglobulinCompetitive Fc receptor blockade
2nd lineRituximab (anti-CD20)~60% response
2nd lineTPO-RAs: romiplostim (SC), eltrombopag (PO)Stimulate platelet production
3rd lineSplenectomy~65% long-term remission
NewerFostamatinib (SYK inhibitor)Refractory ITP
Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)
  • Classic pentad (full pentad rare, do NOT wait for all 5): MAHA + thrombocytopenia + renal dysfunction + neurologic changes + fever
  • Pathophysiology: ADAMTS13 <10% (severely deficient) → ultra-large VWF multimers → platelet aggregation → microvascular thrombosis
  • Smear: Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs)

TTP Treatment — MEDICAL EMERGENCY:

  • Therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) — start URGENTLY (do not wait for ADAMTS13 results)
  • Rituximab (anti-CD20) — reduces relapse
  • Caplacizumab (anti-VWF nanobody) — faster platelet recovery
  • Do NOT delay TPE
  • Do NOT give platelet transfusions — "fuel on the fire"
Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS)
TypeTypical (STEC-HUS)Atypical (aHUS)
CauseE. coli O157:H7 (Shiga toxin)Complement dysregulation
AgeChildrenAny age
PresentationBloody diarrhea → MAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKIMAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKI (no diarrhea)
TreatmentSupportive (NO antibiotics — may worsen)Eculizumab (anti-C5)
Von Willebrand Disease (Table 115-2)
TypeVWF:AgVWF ActivityFVIIIMultimer Pattern
1 (80%)↓↓↓Normal distribution, ↓ quantity
2A↓↓↓↓Loss of high + intermediate MW multimers
2B↓↓↓↓Loss of high MW (↑ platelet binding → thrombocytopenia)
2M↓↓↓↓Normal distribution
2NNormalNormal↓↓Normal (mimics Hemophilia A)
3AbsentAbsent↓↓↓Absent (severe bleeding)

Treatment:

  • Type 1: DDAVP (desmopressin) 0.3 μg/kg IV — releases stored VWF from endothelium (test response first with trial dose)
  • Type 2/3: VWF-containing factor concentrates (Humate-P)
  • Adjunctive: Tranexamic acid (TXA), aminocaproic acid
  • Avoid DDAVP in Type 2B — worsens thrombocytopenia
  • Acquired VWD: Associated with MGUS, MM, Waldenström's, aortic stenosis (Heyde's syndrome = aortic stenosis + GI bleeding from angiodysplasia + acquired VWD)

topics.title: Coagulation Disorders
topics.content:
Inherited Coagulation Disorders (Table 116-1)
FactorDiseaseInheritancePrevalenceTreatmentHalf-life
FVIIIHemophilia AX-linked1:5,000 malesFVIII concentrates8–12h
FIXHemophilia BX-linked1:30,000 malesFIX concentrates18–24h
FibrinogenAfibrinogenemiaAR1:1,000,000Cryoprecipitate2–4 days
FXIHemophilia CAR1:1,000,000FFP40–70h
FXIIIFXIII deficiencyAR1:2,000,000Cryoprecipitate/FXIII concentrate11–14 days
Hemophilia A & B
  • X-linked recessive: Males affected, females are carriers (may have mild bleeding)
  • Severity: Severe (<1% activity), Moderate (1–5%), Mild (6–30%)
  • Clinical hallmark: Hemarthroses (knees, elbows, ankles), deep muscle hematomas, retroperitoneal bleeding
  • Lab: Prolonged aPTT with normal PT and normal bleeding time/platelet count
  • Mixing study: Corrects (factor deficiency, not inhibitor)
Dosing Formulas
  • FVIII dose = (Target% – Baseline%) × weight (kg) × 0.5 units/kg
  • FIX dose = (Target% – Baseline%) × weight (kg) × 1.0 unit/kg
Inhibitors
  • Develop in 30% severe Hem A, 3–5% Hem B
  • Bethesda assay quantifies inhibitor titer
  • Low-responder (<5 BU): High-dose factor may overcome
  • High-responder (≥5 BU): Bypass agents — aPCC (FEIBA) or rFVIIa (NovoSeven)
Emicizumab
  • Bispecific antibody bridging FIXa and FX → mimics FVIII cofactor function
  • SC injection, monthly (or q2wk/q4wk)
  • Works with or without inhibitors
  • Dramatically reduces bleeding episodes in severe Hem A
Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)

Causes (Table 116-2): Sepsis (#1), trauma, obstetric complications (placental abruption, amniotic fluid embolism, HELLP), malignancy (APL, mucin-secreting adenocarcinoma), vascular (aortic aneurysm, giant hemangioma)

ISTH DIC Score (Table 116-3)
ParameterValuePoints
Platelets>100K / 50–100K / <50K0 / 1 / 2
D-dimerNormal / Moderate ↑ / Severe ↑0 / 2 / 3
PT prolongation<3s / 3–6s / >6s0 / 1 / 2
Fibrinogen>1 g/L / <1 g/L0 / 1

Score <5 = low-grade (non-overt) DIC; Score ≥5 = overt DIC

DIC Treatment:

  • Treat the underlying cause (most important)
  • Platelets if <10–20K or actively bleeding
  • FFP/Cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.5 g/dL
  • Heparin: Only in chronic/compensated DIC with predominantly thrombotic features (e.g., Trousseau's syndrome)
Vitamin K Deficiency
  • PT prolonged first (Factor VII has shortest half-life at 4–6 hours)
  • Causes: Malnutrition, antibiotics (gut flora suppression), biliary obstruction, malabsorption, warfarin
  • Treatment: IV vitamin K 10 mg — restores factor levels in 8–10 hours
Coagulation in Liver Disease (Table 116-4)
  • Both bleeding AND thrombosis risk (decreased synthesis of procoagulants AND anticoagulants — protein C, protein S, antithrombin)
  • PT/INR unreliable for assessing bleeding risk in cirrhosis
  • Use 4F-PCC over FFP (lower volume, faster correction)

topics.title: Arterial & Venous Thrombosis
topics.content:
Heritable Thrombophilias (Table 117-1)
ConditionPrevalenceMechanismVTE Risk
Factor V Leiden5–8% CaucasiansAPC resistance3–8× heterozygous, 80× homozygous
Prothrombin G20210A2–3% Caucasians↑ prothrombin levels2–3×
Antithrombin deficiency0.02–0.2%↓ AT activityMost thrombogenic of the three (10–50×)
Protein C deficiency0.2–0.5%↓ inactivation of FVa/FVIIIa7–10×
Protein S deficiencyRare↓ cofactor for protein C5–10×
Hyperhomocysteinemia5–10%MTHFR 677C→T (common), direct endothelial toxicity2–3×
Acquired Causes (Table 117-3)

Surgery, malignancy, antiphospholipid syndrome, trauma, pregnancy/postpartum, OCP/HRT, immobilization, obesity, myeloproliferative disorders, nephrotic syndrome, PNH, inflammatory bowel disease

Key Concepts
  • Virchow's triad: Stasis + endothelial injury + hypercoagulability
  • Cancer patients: 4× increased VTE risk
  • Arterial thrombi: Platelet-rich (white), high shear stress → antiplatelet drugs
  • Venous thrombi: Fibrin-rich (red), low shear → anticoagulants

topics.title: Antithrombotic Drugs
topics.content:
Antiplatelet Drugs
DrugMechanismKey Points
AspirinIrreversible COX-1 inhibition → ↓ TXA₂75–100 mg daily, secondary prevention
ClopidogrelIrreversible P2Y12 inhibition (prodrug, requires CYP2C19)75 mg daily, loading 300–600 mg
PrasugrelIrreversible P2Y12 (prodrug)10 mg daily, more potent than clopidogrel, CI if prior stroke/TIA
TicagrelorReversible P2Y12 (NOT a prodrug)90 mg BID, dyspnea side effect, no CYP2C19 dependence
CangrelorReversible P2Y12 (IV)Rapid onset/offset, peri-PCI
VorapaxarPAR-1 (thrombin receptor) antagonistCI if prior stroke/TIA
AbciximabAnti-GPIIb/IIIa mAbPCI setting
EptifibatideGPIIb/IIIa peptide inhibitorPCI/ACS
TirofibanGPIIb/IIIa non-peptide inhibitorPCI/ACS
Anticoagulants

##### Unfractionated Heparin (UFH)

  • Mechanism: Activates antithrombin → inhibits thrombin (FIIa) + FXa
  • Monitor: aPTT (target 1.5–2.5× control)
  • Reversal: Protamine sulfate (1 mg per 100 units heparin)
  • Side effects: Bleeding, HIT, osteoporosis (long-term)

##### LMWH (Table 118-5)

  • Advantages: SC administration, predictable dose-response, no routine monitoring, lower HIT risk
  • Monitor anti-Xa in: renal impairment, obesity, pregnancy
  • Protamine only partially reverses (~60%)
  • Examples: Enoxaparin, dalteparin, tinzaparin

##### Fondaparinux

  • Synthetic pentasaccharide, anti-Xa only
  • SC, t½ = 17 hours
  • No HIT (does not bind PF4)
  • No specific reversal agent (recombinant FVIIa off-label)

##### Direct Thrombin Inhibitors (Table 118-7)

FeatureArgatrobanBivalirudin
MetabolismHepaticEnzymatic (peptidases)
Half-life45 min25 min
MonitoringaPTTACT
Primary useHIT (especially with renal impairment)HIT + PCI

##### Direct Oral Anticoagulants — DOACs (Table 118-9)

FeatureRivaroxabanApixabanEdoxabanDabigatran
TargetFactor XaFactor XaFactor XaThrombin
ProdrugNoNoNoYes
Bioavailability80%60%50%6%
DosingQD (BID)BIDQDBID
Half-life7–11h12h9–11h12–17h
Renal excretion33%25% (safest in CKD)35%80% (avoid if CrCl <30)
ReversalAndexanet alfaAndexanet alfaAndexanet alfaIdarucizumab

##### Warfarin

  • Mechanism: Inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase → ↓ factors II, VII, IX, X and protein C & S
  • Monitor: INR (target 2.0–3.0; mechanical valves 2.5–3.5)
  • Pharmacogenomics: CYP2C9 and VKORC1 polymorphisms affect dosing
  • Warfarin skin necrosis: Protein C depletion (shortest half-life among vitamin K-dependent proteins) → microvascular thrombosis. Prevention: overlap with heparin for ≥5 days and until INR therapeutic for ≥24h
  • Teratogenic: Contraindicated in pregnancy (use LMWH instead)
  • Reversal: Vitamin K (oral/IV) ± 4F-PCC for emergent reversal (faster than FFP)
Fibrinolytics
AgentFibrin SpecificityHalf-lifeDosing
Alteplase (tPA)Moderate5 minIV infusion (90–100 mg for MI)
TenecteplaseHigh20 minSingle IV bolus (weight-based)
ReteplaseModerate15 minTwo IV boluses 30 min apart
StreptokinaseNone60 min1.5 million units IV over 30–60 min

Indications: Acute STEMI (within 12h if no PCI available), acute ischemic stroke (alteplase within 4.5h), massive PE with hemodynamic instability

Absolute Contraindications: Active internal bleeding, recent (2 months) CVA/head trauma, intracranial neoplasm, suspected aortic dissection, recent major surgery/trauma (<2–3 weeks)

topics.title: Transfusion Medicine
topics.content:
Blood Components (Table 113-1)
ComponentVolumeContentShelf Life
PRBC300 mLRBCs, Hct 55–80%42 days (4°C)
Platelets50 mL each (or 1 apheresis unit ~200–300 mL)≥5.5×10¹⁰ per unit5 days (20–24°C with agitation)
FFP200–250 mLAll coagulation factors1 year (−18°C)
Cryoprecipitate10–15 mLFibrinogen, FVIII, FXIII, VWF, fibronectin1 year (−18°C)
ABO Compatibility (Table 113-2)
  • O = universal donor for RBCs (no A or B antigens)
  • AB = universal donor for plasma (no anti-A or anti-B antibodies)
  • Type and screen required before transfusion
Transfusion Reactions (Table 113-5)
ReactionMechanismFeaturesManagement
Acute hemolyticABO incompatibility (IgM, complement)Fever, flank pain, hemoglobinuria, DIC, shockSTOP transfusion, NS resuscitation, forced diuresis
FNHTRCytokines from donor WBCsFever, chills, rigorsAntipyretics; leukoreduction prevents
AllergicIgE to donor plasma proteinsUrticaria, pruritusAntihistamines; can restart if mild
AnaphylacticAnti-IgA antibodies (IgA-deficient recipient)Hypotension, bronchospasm, angioedemaEpinephrine, use washed RBCs/IgA-deficient products
TRALIDonor anti-HLA/anti-neutrophil antibodiesNon-cardiogenic pulmonary edema (ARDS) within 6hSupportive care (no diuretics)
TACOVolume overloadPulmonary edema, hypertension, ↑JVPDiuretics, slow transfusion rate
Delayed hemolyticAnamnestic antibody responseFalling Hb 3–14 days post-transfusionUsually mild, monitor; repeat crossmatch
TA-GVHDDonor T-cells attack recipient tissuesPancytopenia, rash, hepatitis, diarrhea>90% fatal; irradiation of products prevents
Clinical Pearl — TRALI vs TACO:
FeatureTRALITACO
BNPNormal↑
PCWPNormal↑
FeverYesNo
Response to diureticsNoYes

topics.title: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation
topics.content:
Types of HCT
TypeSourceUse
AutologousPatient's own HSCsMyeloma, lymphoma (no GVHD risk)
AllogeneicDonor: matched sibling, MUD, haploidenticalAML, ALL, MDS, AA (GVL effect)
SyngeneicIdentical twinNo GVHD, no GVL
Conditioning Regimens
TypeIntensityRegimensIndications
MyeloablativeHighBuCy (busulfan + cyclophosphamide), TBI + CyYounger, fit patients
Reduced-intensity (RIC)LowerFludarabine-basedOlder patients, comorbidities
Graft-versus-Host Disease (GVHD)

##### Acute GVHD (classically within 100 days)

OrganManifestation
SkinMaculopapular rash (palms/soles early)
Liver↑ Bilirubin, hepatitis
GIWatery/bloody diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain
  • Prophylaxis: Calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus or cyclosporine) + methotrexate or mycophenolate
  • Treatment: Methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg; steroid-refractory → ruxolitinib

##### Chronic GVHD (typically >100 days)

  • Sclerodermatous skin changes, sicca syndrome (dry eyes/mouth), bronchiolitis obliterans, oral lichenoid changes, fasciitis, hepatitis
  • Resembles autoimmune diseases (Sjögren's, systemic sclerosis)
Post-HCT Infection Timeline (Table 114-3)
PhaseTimeKey PathogensPrevention
Pre-engraftment0–30 daysBacteria (gram-neg/pos), Candida, HSVFluoroquinolone, fluconazole, acyclovir
Post-engraftment30–100 daysCMV, Aspergillus, PCPCMV monitoring/preemptive ganciclovir, TMP-SMX
Late>100 daysVZV, encapsulated bacteria, PCPAcyclovir, TMP-SMX, revaccination
5-Year Survival after Allogeneic HCT (Table 114-4)
Disease5-Year Survival
AML (CR1)50–60%
ALL (CR1)50–55%
CML (chronic phase)>70%
MDS~40%
Aplastic anemia (matched sibling)80–90%

### Quick-Reference: Anemia Classification by MCV

MCVDifferential
Microcytic (<80)Iron deficiency, thalassemia, ACD, sideroblastic, lead poisoning
Normocytic (80–100)ACD, CKD, acute blood loss, hemolysis, mixed deficiency, BM failure
Macrocytic (>100)B12/folate deficiency, MDS, liver disease, hypothyroidism, drugs (hydroxyurea, MTX), reticulocytosis

Study guide compiled from Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 21st Edition — Chapters 96–118. For examination preparation purposes.
